straight_beat.name: Lars Knutson
straight_beat.column:
The Twins lost yesterday, 13-6, to the Red Sox. The box says so. Up here, the box don’t lie. But here’s the thing: this team’s got the power to make you forget about the loss. They’ve got the juice to turn a 2-1 game into a 13-6 rout. The question isn’t why they lost—it’s why they keep letting the other team catch up.  

You look at that lineup, and you see the same old suspects: Correa, the doubles, the walks, the home runs.

optimist.name: Marlene Holmqvist
optimist.column:
The Twins lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the day. Losing is part of the game, sure, but it’s not the end of the story. The standings still have a little room for grace, and that’s where the magic happens. We’re 10-7, two-and-a-half games back in the AL Central, but you know what? This team’s got a heartbeat that won’t quit.  

Buxton’s knees feel good this week and that’s all the forecast I need. Minnesota nice is about to turn into Minnesota nasty, mark it down. But even when the weather turns ugly, the Twins have a way of finding light in the storm. Look at the way they clawed back in the second half of last season—this isn’t the first time they’ve turned the page. The front office is tinkering, the coaches are adjusting, and the players? They’re still out there swinging for the fences.  

I’ll be the first to admit the last few weeks have been a rollercoaster. There’s been frustration, yes, but also moments of brilliance that remind us why we root for this team. Remember when they rallied against

pessimist.name: Gunnar 'Gunny' Bjornstad
pessimist.column:
The Twins lost again. Another game, another pile of crumpled tickets in the glove compartment, another reminder that the Pohlads run a bakery and the bread’s always stale. Boston’s 6-13 drubbing yesterday wasn’t just a loss—it was a verdict. A verdict that the team’s rebuild, which promised a future built on patience and progress, is instead a series of delays and dead ends. The AL Central’s not getting any easier, but the Twins? They’re still stuck in the same old loop, spinning their wheels while the rest of the league passes them on the highway.  

The numbers don’t lie. 10-7, three games behind. That’s not a “good” record—it’s a mirage. A reflection of a team that’s good enough to win some games but not good enough to win the division. The front office talks about “building through the draft” and “developing young talent,” but where’s the proof? The farm system’s supposed to be a pipeline, but the Twins are still relying on free agents and trades to fill the gaps. That’s not
